summary: This advice column covers several topics: a critique of three dating/romance books that oversimplify complex social dynamics, advice on high-frequency trading controversies (dismissing Flash Boys as relatively unimportant compared to larger financial crimes), encouragement to apply for multiple academic positions at the same institution, commentary on American hero worship and inequality, and guidance for mentoring a young man with unrealistic life plans. Throughout, Aunt Pythia offers pragmatic, sometimes cynical takes on modern social and economic problems.
key_points: Dating and romance books tend to oversimplify complex social dynamics like gender economics and sexual identity
High-frequency trading scandals, while real, are minor compared to systemic financial crimes like the 2008 crisis - 'business as usual' in finance
Harvard Business School alumni recognize inequality as a major problem but propose solutions (tax reform, deregulation) that would actually worsen it
Hero worship and 'winner take all' mentality has eliminated support structures and forced workers to do multiple jobs
Individual human stories drive social change more than statistics - we need stories that represent systemic issues rather than aspirational hero worship
money_quotes: Lewis is a great writer, and he tells a great story, but this time I think he just kind of randomly chose the good guys and bad guys and convinced everyone something terrible was going on because it sells books, when in fact it's business as usual in the world of high frequency trading.
It's obviously terrible to only rely on your sex appeal, especially as you age and are suddenly unfuckable.
If you are the only person stopping to smell the roses, you will get trampled from behind. It's a collective action problem, and a cultural problem.
The way humans work is we care about individual stories.
What we need to do is make sure the individual stories we hear about are examples of larger issues important to a lot of people, rather than just aspirational hero-worshipping schlock.
key_insight: Systemic problems in finance and society persist because we focus on dramatic individual stories rather than the mundane, larger-scale issues that actually harm people.
